does: This paper introduces MINT, a benchmark designed to evaluate large language models in multi-turn interactive scenarios involving both tool use and natural language feedback. The core contribution is a systematic evaluation framework where LLMs can execute Python code to access tools and receive simulated user feedback from GPT-4 across multiple conversation rounds. The authors repurpose established datasets from reasoning, coding, and decision-making domains, carefully curating them into a compact evaluation set. They evaluate 20 open and closed-source LLMs to assess how well these models handle iterative problem-solving that requires tool interaction and responsiveness to natural language corrections or guidance, moving beyond single-turn benchmark evaluations toward more realistic interactive scenarios.
connects: This work responds to a significant gap in LLM evaluation methodology. Prior benchmarks have focused heavily on single-turn performance metrics and static task completion, following the dominant paradigm of leaderboard-based evaluation. However, emerging research has highlighted the importance of multi-turn interactions and tool use in real-world LLM applications, building on earlier work in agent-based systems and interactive AI. The paper builds on foundational ideas about integrating language models with external tools and extends recent work on in-context learning and instruction-following by emphasizing the dynamic nature of user feedback. It addresses critiques that standard benchmarks insufficiently capture how humans actually interact with LLMs through iterative refinement and correction.
enables: This benchmark establishes infrastructure for evaluating the next generation of interactive LLM capabilities. By providing a reproducible framework for multi-turn evaluation with tool execution and simulated feedback, it enables researchers to systematically study LLM robustness, error recovery, and instruction-following under realistic conditions. The work points toward developing LLMs that can gracefully handle corrections, adapt their strategies based on feedback, and maintain context across extended conversations. It also opens directions for studying how different types of natural language feedback affect model behavior and performance. Future work could extend this framework to human-in-the-loop evaluation, more complex tool ecosystems, and longer conversation horizons, ultimately supporting the development of more reliable AI assistants for real-world deployment.
